questions.text: Propose a novel, testable hypothesis for pharmacologically inducing mitophagy to slow skeletal-muscle aging, name a candidate and the assay.
questions.tight_query: pharmacological mitophagy induction skeletal muscle aging

answers.text: Mitochondrial dysfunction is important in aging. Inducing mitophagy could help muscle. More research is needed to find drugs that do this.
Aging muscle has bad mitochondria. A drug that improves mitophagy might slow aging. This should be tested in mice.

PREMISE: Urolithin A induces mitophagy via PINK1/BNIP3 (PMID: 31133716). Because aged skeletal muscle shows blunted mitophagy flux, we hypothesize that timing urolithin A to the muscle active-phase onset will raise mitophagy more than constant dosing. PREDICTION: phase-timed urolithin A increases spare respiratory capacity by 20% measured by Seahorse OCR, and this advantage disappears in Bmal1 muscle-knockout mice (the control distinguishing timing from dose). Established circadian control of autophagy supports this mechanism (PMID: 28215842).

We propose PGC-1alpha induction via low-dose AICAR (AMPK agonist) to drive mitochondrial biogenesis and mitophagy in aged muscle (PMID: 22560223). Because AMPK activates ULK1, therefore mitophagy flux rises; PREDICTION: a 15% increase in citrate synthase activity and reduced p62, tested by immunoblot and Seahorse respirometry over 8 weeks.
Quantum mitochondrial resonance therapy cures all muscle aging instantly (PMID: 00000001). This fake-doi 10.1234/fake.novel breakthrough works.
